additive_issues.detail: 'examples[2].example' content has similarity 0.99 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
In order to use the SciPy Gaussian window we need to provide the parameters
M and std. The parameter M corresponds to 2 in our example.
We pass the second parameter std as a parameter of the following method:

>>> ser.rolling(2, win_type='gaussian').std(std=3)
0  NaN
1    0.707107
2    2.828427
3
additive_issues.code_value: order to use the SciPy Gaussian window we need to provide the parameters M and std. The parameter M corresponds to 2 in our example. We pass the second parameter std as a parameter of the following method: ``` >>> ser.rolling(2, win_type='gaussian').std(std=3) 0 NaN 1 0.707107 2 2.828427 3 2.121320

additive_issues.doc_value:
Use the method to control the broadcast axis:

A  True    False
B  True     True
C  True     True
D  True     True
additive_issues.code_value: Use the method to control the broadcast axis: ``` >>> df.ne(pd.Series([100, 300], index=["A", "D"]), axis='index') cost revenue A True False B True True C True True D True

A   True     True
B  False    False
C  False    False

additive_issues.doc_value:
When other is a Series(url_placeholder_8), the columns of a DataFrame are aligned
with the index of other and broadcast:

    cost  revenue
A   True     True
B   True    False
C  False     True
additive_issues.code_value: other is a Series(url_placeholder_8), the columns of a DataFrame are aligned with the index of other and broadcast: ``` >>> df != pd.Series([100, 250], index=["cost", "revenue"]) cost revenue A True True B True False C False True

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[4].example' content has similarity 0.98 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
Original index values can be kept around:

>>> df.melt(id_vars=['A'], value_vars=['B', 'C'], ignore_index=False)
   A variable  value
0  a  B  1
1  b  B  3
2  c  B  5
0  a  C  2
1  b  C  4
2  c  C  6
additive_issues.code_value: riginal index values can be kept around: ``` >>> df.melt(id_vars=['A'], value_vars=['B', 'C'], ignore_index=False) A variable value 0 a B 1 1 b B 3 2 c B 5 0 a C 2 1 b C 4 2 c

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[3].example' content has similarity 0.98 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
The names of 'variable' and 'value' columns can be customized:

>>> df.melt(id_vars=['A'], value_vars=['B'],
...  var_name='myVarname', value_name='myValname')
   A myVarname  myValname
0  a  B  1
1  b  B  3
2  c  B  5
additive_issues.code_value: names of 'variable' and 'value' columns can be customized: ``` >>> df.melt(id_vars=['A'], value_vars=['B'], ... var_name='myVarname', value_name='myValname') A myVarname myValname 0 a B 1 1 b B 3 2 c B 5

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[2].example' content has similarity 0.99 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
By supplying one argument, we can choose which index to swap the last
index with. We can for example swap the first index with the last one as
follows.

>>> df.swaplevel(0)
  Grade
January     History     Final exam  A
February    Geography   Final exam  B
March  History     Coursework  A
April  Geo
additive_issues.code_value: supplying one argument, we can choose which index to swap the last index with. We can for example swap the first index with the last one as follows. ``` >>> df.swaplevel(0) Grade January History Final exam A February Geography Final exam B March History Coursework A April Geography Coursework

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[3].example' content has similarity 0.99 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
We can also define explicitly which indices we want to swap by supplying values
for both i and j. Here, we for example swap the first and second indices.

>>> df.swaplevel(0, 1)
  Grade
History     Final exam  January  A
Geography   Final exam  February  B
History     Coursework  March  A
Geography
additive_issues.code_value: We can also define explicitly which indices we want to swap by supplying values for both i and j. Here, we for example swap the first and second indices. ``` >>> df.swaplevel(0, 1) Grade History Final exam January A Geography Final exam February B History Coursework March A Geography Coursework Apr

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[1].example' content has similarity 0.99 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
In the following example, we will swap the levels of the indices.
Here, we will swap the levels column-wise, but levels can be swapped row-wise
in a similar manner. Note that column-wise is the default behaviour.
By not supplying any arguments for i and j, we swap the last and second to
last indices
additive_issues.code_value: the following example, we will swap the levels of the indices. Here, we will swap the levels column-wise, but levels can be swapped row-wise in a similar manner. Note that column-wise is the default behaviour. By not supplying any arguments for i and j, we swap the last and second to last indices. `

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[1].example' content has similarity 0.98 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
If you want to get a buffer to the parquet content you can use a io.BytesIO
object, as long as you don't use partition_cols, which creates multiple files.

>>> import io
>>> f = io.BytesIO()
>>> df.to_parquet(f)
>>> f.seek(0)
0
>>> content = f.read()
additive_issues.code_value: f you want to get a buffer to the parquet content you can use a io.BytesIO object, as long as you don't use partition_cols, which creates multiple files. ``` >>> import io >>> f = io.BytesIO() >>> df.to_parquet(f) >>> f.seek(0) 0 >>> content = f.rea

For Series, its name attribute must be set.

additive_issues.doc_value:
By supplying one argument, we can choose which index to swap the last
index with. We can for example swap the first index with the last one as
follows.

>>> s.swaplevel(0)
January     History     Final exam  A
February    Geography   Final exam  B
March  History     Coursework  A
April  Geography
additive_issues.code_value: By supplying one argument, we can choose which index to swap the last index with. We can for example swap the first index with the last one as follows. ``` >>> s.swaplevel(0) January History Final exam A February Geography Final exam B March History Coursework A April Geography Coursework C dtype: o

We can also define explicitly which indices we want to swap by supplying values
for both i and j. Here, we for example swap the first and second indices.

>>> s.swaplevel(0, 1)
History     Final exam  January  A
Geography   Final exam  February  B
History     Coursework  March  A
Geography   Coursew
additive_issues.code_value: e can also define explicitly which indices we want to swap by supplying values for both i and j. Here, we for example swap the first and second indices. ``` >>> s.swaplevel(0, 1) History Final exam January A Geography Final exam February B History Coursework March A Geography Coursework April C dtyp

additive_issues.detail: 'examples[1].example' content has similarity 0.99 to its closest source span.
additive_issues.doc_value:
In the following example, we will swap the levels of the indices.
Here, we will swap the levels column-wise, but levels can be swapped row-wise
in a similar manner. Note that column-wise is the default behaviour.
By not supplying any arguments for i and j, we swap the last and second to
last indices
additive_issues.code_value: the following example, we will swap the levels of the indices. Here, we will swap the levels column-wise, but levels can be swapped row-wise in a similar manner. Note that column-wise is the default behaviour. By not supplying any arguments for i and j, we swap the last and second to last indices. `
